claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 4 (under 16); Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 14 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about your child's age.**

How old is the child you're claiming Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know which age category applies to your child. If you have multiple children, we can go through each one separately.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to claim Child Benefit. I have two children actually - Alex who is 4 years old, and Blake who is 14.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Wonderful! Thank you for letting me know about Alex (4 years old) and Blake (14 years old). Since both children are under 16, they automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit.

Let me continue the assessment for both of them. Since they're in the same age category, I can ask the questions for both together.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are Alex and Blake both working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Given that Alex is 4 and Blake is 14, I expect neither is in full-time employment, but I just need to confirm this for the assessment. 

Is it correct that neither child is working 24 or more hours per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, neither of them work at all. Alex is only 4, so obviously not working, and Blake doesn't have a job either.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That makes perfect sense - thank you for confirming. Let me continue with the next question.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

Specifically, this means neither child is personally claiming benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in their own name.

Can you confirm that neither Alex nor Blake is receiving these kinds of benefits in their own right?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are Alex and Blake in any apprenticeships?**

Given their ages (4 and 14), I wouldn't expect so, but I need to confirm. If either child is in an apprenticeship, the rules vary by region:

- **England**: All apprenticeships disqualify
- **Scotland**: Modern Apprenticeships disqualify
- **Wales**: Foundation Apprenticeships are approved, but other apprenticeships disqualify

Can you confirm that neither Alex nor Blake is currently in an apprenticeship?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are in apprenticeships. Alex is only 4 and Blake is 14, so they're both too young for that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you for confirming! Now I need to check your residency status.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify through **any one** of the following:

1. **Normally live in the UK** - You are a resident of the UK
2. **Settled status** - You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Pre-settled status and working** - You have pre-settled status and are working/earning above the National Insurance threshold
4. **Pre-settled status and seeking work** - You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work
5. **Pre-settled status with sufficient resources** - You have pre-settled status and can financially support yourself and family
6. **Pre-settled status and studying** - You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. **Pre-settled status as a family member** - You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Crown servant abroad** - You are living abroad as a Crown servant

Do any of these apply to you? For most people, the first option (normally living in the UK) is the most common.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?**

You can be considered responsible through **any one** of the following:

1. **Living with the child** - Alex and/or Blake live with you
2. **Contributing to their upkeep** - You pay at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards their care (e.g., food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents)
3. **Paying maintenance** - You make maintenance payments under a court order or agreement

Do any of these apply to you for both Alex and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with me at home. Alex doesn't live with me, but I help with their upkeep - I contribute money towards their care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. That's helpful to know:

- **Blake (14)**: Lives with you ✓
- **Alex (4)**: You contribute to their upkeep ✓

Both of these satisfy the responsibility requirement. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is anyone else currently claiming or planning to claim Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

This is important because only one person can receive Child Benefit for each child at a time. 

- **If no one else is claiming** for either child, we can proceed
- **If someone else is claiming** but you've agreed between yourselves who should receive it, we can also proceed
- **If there's a dispute** about who should claim, HMRC will need to make the decision

What's the situation for each child?
- **Blake** (who lives with you)
- **Alex** (who doesn't live with you, but you contribute to their upkeep)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
No one else is claiming for Blake - he lives with me.

For Alex, they don't live with me, so I need to tell you more about that situation. How much do I need to contribute for it to count?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great question! Let me check the specific requirements for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good question! To count as "contributing to a child's upkeep," **you need to pay at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment** (or the equivalent in kind) towards Alex's care.

Contributions can include things like:
- Food
- Clothes
- Pocket money
- Housing costs
- Presents

These can be paid weekly, monthly, or as lump sum payments.

The current Child Benefit rates aren't specified in my documents, but you can check the latest rates at [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit). As a general guide, it's approximately £25 per week for a first/only child and around £17 per week for additional children.

**So for Alex:**
1. Do you contribute at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards Alex's care?
2. Is anyone else currently claiming (or planning to claim) Child Benefit for Alex - for example, the person Alex lives with?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I contribute £50 a week towards Alex's care, so that should be enough. And no, the person Alex lives with isn't claiming Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great - £50 per week is well above the Child Benefit rate, so your contribution definitely counts. And since no one else is claiming for Alex, we can proceed with the assessment for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Where are Alex and Blake currently living?**

I need to check if any special circumstances apply. For each child, please let me know if they are:

1. **In hospital**
2. **In local authority care**
3. **In a fostering arrangement**
4. **Living normally** (at home or with family/guardians in a standard living situation)

For **Blake** (lives with you) - I assume this is a normal living situation at home?

For **Alex** (doesn't live with you) - Where does Alex live? Is it a normal living situation with another family member or guardian?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Blake lives at home with me - that's a normal living situation.

Alex lives with their other parent - also a normal living situation, not in hospital, care, or fostering.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for confirming - both children are in normal living situations. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the accommodation or maintenance for Alex or Blake?**

You generally cannot receive Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child's accommodation or upkeep.

Can you confirm that the local council is not paying towards either:
- **Blake's** accommodation or maintenance, or
- **Alex's** accommodation or maintenance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - the council isn't paying anything towards either of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That completes the assessment. Based on all the information you've provided, I can now give you the outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Great News - You're Eligible for Child Benefit!

Based on the information you've provided, **you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake**.

### Summary for Each Child:

**Alex (4 years old)** ✅ Eligible
- Under 16, so automatically meets age requirement
- Not working or receiving benefits
- You're contributing £50/week towards their care (exceeds the threshold)
- No one else is claiming for Alex
- Local council is not paying for their care

**Blake (14 years old)** ✅ Eligible
- Under 16, so automatically meets age requirement
- Not working or receiving benefits
- Lives with you, so you're clearly responsible
- No one else is claiming for Blake
- Local council is not paying for their care

---

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and both children
3. **Provide birth certificates** (or adoption certificates) for Alex and Blake
4. **Payments** are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### Important Note:
⚠️ **High Income Child Benefit Charge**: If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay back some or all of the benefit through a tax charge. You can still claim, but you should be aware of this.

For more information and to apply, visit: [gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex (4 years old) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) They are under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically; (2) They are not working; (3) They are not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) They are not in an apprenticeship; (5) You meet UK residency requirements (normally living in the UK); (6) You are responsible for Alex by contributing £50 per week towards their upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit rate; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex is in a normal living situation with their other parent; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (14 years old) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) They are under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically; (2) They are not working; (3) They are not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) They are not in an apprenticeship; (5) You meet UK residency requirements (normally living in the UK); (6) You are responsible for Blake as they live with you; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake; (8) Blake is in a normal living situation at home with you; (9) The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children. They meet all the key requirements: they live in the UK, both children are under 16, neither child is working or receiving benefits, neither is in an apprenticeship, and the local council is not paying for either child. The claimant has responsibility for both children - Blake lives with them directly, and they contribute £50 per week towards Alex's care (which exceeds the Child Benefit threshold). No one else is claiming Child Benefit for either child.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location

Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (4 years old) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) They are under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically; (2) They are not working; (3) They are not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) They are not in an apprenticeship; (5) You meet UK residency requirements (normally living in the UK); (6) You are responsible for Alex by contributing £50 per week towards their upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit rate; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex is in a normal living situation with their other parent; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (14 years old) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) They are under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically; (2) They are not working; (3) They are not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) They are not in an apprenticeship; (5) You meet UK residency requirements (normally living in the UK); (6) You are responsible for Blake as they live with you; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake; (8) Blake is in a normal living situation at home with you; (9) The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children. They meet all the key requirements: they live in the UK, both children are under 16, neither child is working or receiving benefits, neither is in an apprenticeship, and the local council is not paying for either child. The claimant has responsibility for both children - Blake lives with them directly, and they contribute £50 per week towards Alex's care (which exceeds the Child Benefit threshold). No one else is claiming Child Benefit for either child.
